prompt.text: The chromosomal basis of inheritance, which links the behavior of chromosomes during meiosis to Mendelian inheritance patterns, explains that __.

options: Genes are located at specific loci on chromosomes and segregate independently during meiosis
Alleles are always inherited in the same combinations as they appeared in the parents
Chromosomes are only responsible for determining the sex of an offspring
DNA replication occurs twice during the meiotic cell cycle to ensure genetic diversity
questions.answer: Genes are located at specific loci on chromosomes and segregate independently during meiosis
questions.explanation: Sutton and Boveri proposed that Mendelian factors are genes on chromosomes whose segregation during meiosis explains inheritance ratios. Linked genes on the same chromosome are an exception, not proof that alleles always stay in parental combinations.

prompt.text: In genetic studies, the phenotype that is most commonly observed in natural populations is referred to as the ________, whereas alternative traits resulting from changes in the genome are called ________.

questions.explanation: Wild type is the most common phenotype in nature; mutant phenotypes arise from genetic changes relative to that standard. Dominant/recessive describes allele behavior, not the reference phenotype used in lab strains.

prompt.text: In his famous experiments with fruit flies (Drosophila melanogaster), Thomas Hunt Morgan identified the first white-eyed mutant and discovered that it was inherited uniquely by male offspring. This led him to conclude that the gene for eye color was located on which chromosome?

options: The Y chromosome, because only males exhibited the trait
The X chromosome, supporting the theory of sex-linkage
An autosome, since both males and females have eye color
The mitochondrial DNA, inherited only from the mother
questions.answer: The X chromosome, supporting the theory of sex-linkage
questions.explanation: White eyes appeared mainly in males because the recessive allele was X-linked—males inherit a single X and express whatever allele it carries. The Y chromosome carries few genes and does not explain an eye-color trait passed through females to sons.

prompt.text: The majority of sex-linked human genes are X-linked rather than Y-linked because __.

options: The X chromosome undergoes crossing over with the Y chromosome across its entire length
The Y chromosome is primarily composed of non-coding repetitive DNA and contains very few functional loci
X-linked genes are required for the development of both biological sexes during early embryogenesis
The X chromosome is significantly larger and contains a vastly higher number of gene loci than the Y chromosome
questions.answer: The X chromosome is significantly larger and contains a vastly higher number of gene loci than the Y chromosome
questions.explanation: The X chromosome carries hundreds of protein-coding genes, while the Y chromosome has only a small number of functional loci. The Y is gene-poor, but the primary reason most sex-linked traits are X-linked is simply that the X carries far more genes.

prompt.text: In humans, why is it typically only biological females who can be homozygous recessive for an X-linked trait?

options: Biological males lack a second X chromosome and are therefore hemizygous for all X-linked loci.
The Y chromosome contains dominant alleles that always mask recessive traits on the X chromosome.
X-inactivation prevents biological males from expressing recessive alleles on their single X chromosome.
Recessive alleles on the X chromosome are lethal in the embryonic stage for biological males.
questions.answer: Biological males lack a second X chromosome and are therefore hemizygous for all X-linked loci.
questions.explanation: Homozygous recessive requires two copies of the recessive allele; females (XX) can carry aa, but males (XY) have only one X and express whatever allele it carries. X-inactivation occurs in females, not males, and the Y does not generally mask all X-linked recessives.

prompt.text: Because human males possess only a single X chromosome, they cannot be described as homozygous or heterozygous for X-linked genes, but are instead described as __.

questions.explanation: Hemizygous means having only one copy of a gene at a locus—males have a single X and no matching allele on the Y. Haploid refers to chromosome number in gametes; aneuploid and monozygotic describe different concepts entirely.

prompt.text: During embryonic development in female mammals, one of the two X chromosomes in each cell is randomly and permanently inactivated, condensing into a compact object known as a(n) __.

questions.explanation: The inactivated X chromosome is visible as a dense Barr body in interphase nuclei of female mammalian cells. Centrosomes organize spindles; nucleoli make ribosomes; autosomes are non-sex chromosomes.

prompt.text: The tortoiseshell pattern in cats is a visible result of X-inactivation. This mosaic phenotype occurs because __.

options: Heterozygous females consist of different cell lineages, each expressing a different X-linked allele depending on which Barr body formed.
The Y chromosome in male cats carries a suppressor gene that prevents the expression of the orange fur allele.
The Barr body remains active in skin cells but is inactivated in all other somatic tissues of the cat.
Crossing over between the X and Y chromosomes during mitosis creates patches of different genetic material.
questions.answer: Heterozygous females consist of different cell lineages, each expressing a different X-linked allele depending on which Barr body formed.
questions.explanation: Tortoiseshell cats are X-linked heterozygotes for coat color; random X-inactivation in different cell patches produces orange and black fur regions. Males are rarely tortoiseshell because they usually have only one X-linked color allele.

prompt.text: Which of the following best distinguishes between linked genes and sex-linked genes?

options: Linked genes are located on the same chromosome, while sex-linked genes are located specifically on a sex chromosome.
Linked genes are always inherited together, whereas sex-linked genes only appear in one biological sex.
Linked genes are found only on autosomes, while sex-linked genes are found only on the Y chromosome.
Linked genes result from the fusion of gametes, whereas sex-linked genes result from X-inactivation.
questions.answer: Linked genes are located on the same chromosome, while sex-linked genes are located specifically on a sex chromosome.
questions.explanation: Linkage refers to physical proximity on the same chromosome; sex-linkage refers to location on X or Y. Linked genes can recombine if far apart, and sex-linked genes on the X affect both sexes (though expression differs).

prompt.text: In a dihybrid testcross involving linked genes, 'recombinant types' are offspring that exhibit __.

options: The exact same phenotype combinations as either of the P generation parents
New combinations of traits that differ from those observed in either P generation parent
Only the dominant phenotypes for both traits being studied
A 3:1 phenotypic ratio regardless of the distance between the gene loci
questions.answer: New combinations of traits that differ from those observed in either P generation parent
questions.explanation: Recombinant offspring show non-parental trait combinations produced by crossing over between linked genes. Parental types match the original P phenotypes; recombinants arise only when crossovers shuffle alleles onto new chromosomes.

prompt.text: Nondisjunction is a chromosomal error that occurs when ________, leading to an abnormal number of chromosomes in the resulting daughter cells.

options: DNA polymerase fails to replicate a specific segment of a chromosome during S phase
Homologous chromosomes or sister chromatids fail to separate properly during meiosis or mitosis
A segment of a chromosome breaks off and reattaches to a non-homologous chromosome
The nuclear envelope fails to break down during prophase, preventing spindle attachment
questions.answer: Homologous chromosomes or sister chromatids fail to separate properly during meiosis or mitosis
questions.explanation: Nondisjunction is a failure of chromosome separation at anaphase, producing cells with missing or extra whole chromosomes. Translocation involves broken segments reattaching elsewhere; replication errors and envelope defects are different mechanisms.

prompt.text: T/F: Nondisjunction, the failure of chromosomes to separate properly, can occur during both Anaphase I and Anaphase II of meiosis.

questions.explanation: In Anaphase I, homologous pairs may fail to separate; in Anaphase II, sister chromatids may fail to separate. Either error yields aneuploid gametes with the wrong chromosome number.

prompt.text: Aneuploidy is a condition characterized by __.

options: The presence of three or more complete sets of chromosomes in all somatic cells
Having one extra or one missing chromosome relative to the normal diploid number
A chromosomal translocation where a segment moves to a non-homologous chromosome
The successful separation of all homologous pairs during both meiotic divisions
questions.answer: Having one extra or one missing chromosome relative to the normal diploid number
questions.explanation: Aneuploidy means an abnormal number of a specific chromosome (such as trisomy 21 or monosomy X). Three or more complete sets describes polyploidy; translocation is a structural rearrangement, not a count error.

prompt.text: If a human gamete missing a specific chromosome fuses with a normal haploid gamete, the resulting ________ zygote will have ________ chromosomes in total.

questions.explanation: A gamete missing one chromosome (n − 1) fused with a normal gamete (n) yields a zygote with one fewer than the diploid 46—monosomic with 45 chromosomes. Trisomic with 47 would result from an extra chromosome in the gamete, not a missing one.

prompt.text: A fragment of a chromosome breaks off and subsequently joins a non-homologous chromosome. This specific structural alteration is known as a(n) __.

questions.explanation: Translocation moves a chromosomal segment to a non-homologous chromosome. Deletion removes material; duplication repeats a segment; inversion reattaches a fragment in reverse orientation on the same chromosome.

prompt.text: During meiosis, a chromosomal fragment is lost after a breakage occurs, resulting in a chromosome that is missing certain genes. This structural change is called a(n) __.

questions.explanation: Deletion removes a chromosomal segment entirely after breakage. Duplication adds an extra copy; translocation moves the fragment elsewhere; inversion reinserts it flipped on the same chromosome.

prompt.text: When a chromosomal fragment breaks off and fails to rejoin its original chromosome, but instead attaches to its sister chromatid or homologous partner, which two structural alterations occur simultaneously?

questions.explanation: The chromosome that loses the fragment undergoes deletion while the recipient chromosome gains a duplicated segment. This paired loss-and-gain is a classic outcome when a fragment reattaches to a homolog or sister chromatid instead of its original site.

prompt.text: An error during meiosis results in a chromosomal segment being repeated, such that a chromosome carries an extra copy of a specific set of genes. This structural alteration is known as a(n) __.

questions.explanation: Duplication means a region of the chromosome is present in an extra copy. Deletion is loss of a segment; inversion reverses orientation; translocation moves a segment to another chromosome.

prompt.text: A chromosomal fragment breaks off and reattaches to its original chromosome, but in the reverse orientation. This structural change is known as a(n) __.

questions.explanation: Inversion reinserts a broken segment backward on the same chromosome. Deletion removes it; duplication copies it; translocation attaches it to a different, non-homologous chromosome.
